name: American Indian Association of Illinois (AIAI)
description: Founded 2007; Native-governed, Native-serving nonprofit dedicated to transforming Chicago American Indian communities through education, leadership development, culture, history, social justice, and policy reform. Provides academic/social support and financial planning to students and families. Utilizes community volunteers working with youth, elders, and families.
verification_evidence: Working with Illinois Humanities for America 250 programming in 2026.

name: American Indian Center of Chicago (AIC)
description: Established 1953 — the first urban Indian Center in the country. Founded to support Natives who relocated to Chicago under the U.S. government's relocation program. Today offers cultural, social, and educational services including monthly community potlucks, senior lunches, weekly drum practice, open beadwork classes (Tue & Thu 4–7PM), and gallery programming.
verification_evidence: Hosted community gatherings such as the Sweetheart Round Dance in February 2026.

name: American Indian Health Service of Chicago (AIHSC)
description: Federally qualified health center providing culturally competent healthcare to the American Indian/Alaska Native community and other underserved populations since 1975. Services include family practice, behavioral health (therapy, peer support, talking circles), diabetes program, HIV testing, domestic violence prevention, suicide prevention, and substance use disorder treatment.
verification_evidence: Recognized as a grant recipient in June 2026.

name: CPS American Indian Education Program
description: Chicago Public Schools program ensuring American Indian and Alaskan Native students receive appropriate educational services. Provides family resource centers, cultural programming, youth ambassador opportunities, and support services. Operates the Citywide American Indian Education Council (CAIEC) and coordinates language/cultural programming partnerships.
verification_evidence: Program was subject to a fraud investigation in Jan 2026 regarding funding, but historically maintains community presence.

name: Chi-Nations Youth Council (CNYC)
description: Founded 2012; diverse group of Native youth and adults with a mission to create a supportive, open environment for Native youth, raise awareness of cultural identity, and promote healthy lifestyles through arts, activism, and education. Led advocacy against race-based mascots, participated in Standing Rock movement, runs the First Nations Garden, and facilitates medicine walks and regalia-making.
verification_evidence: Awarded the Indigenous Peoples' Social Justice Award in 2025.

name: First Nations Garden
description: Chicago's first Indigenous-led community garden, created in spring 2019 by Chi-Nations Youth Council on a 99-year urban land trust for the Native community. Located at the SE corner of Wilson Ave & N. Pulaski Rd in Albany Park. Provides a healing space for the intertribal Native community, promotes knowledge of Chicago's Indigenous landscape, native plants, and traditional ecological practices.
verification_evidence: Continued to receive municipal support and mutual aid grants as of April 2026.

name: Gichigamiin Indigenous Nations Museum
description: Formerly the Mitchell Museum of the American Indian (founded 1977); renamed Gichigamiin in 2024 as it became Native-led, shifting focus to Chicago's Native community and the tribal nations of the Great Lakes region. One of a handful of U.S. museums focusing exclusively on histories, cultures, and arts of Indigenous peoples. Actively recruits volunteer docents.
verification_evidence: Living Stories exhibition on display through June 2026.

name: Indigenous Chicago
description: Multifaceted collaboration between the Newberry Library, Chicago American Indian Community, and tribal nations with ancestral ties to Chicago. Produces interactive digital maps, temporary exhibitions, curriculum for high school social studies students, and public programming. Documents present and future Indigenous community presence across the city.
verification_evidence: Physical exhibition at Newberry ended Jan 2025, but digital resources and the project continue.

name: Native American Chamber of Commerce of Illinois (NACC-IL)
description: Provides business education, mentoring, and networking opportunities for Native American business owners and entrepreneurs throughout Illinois. Maintains a comprehensive directory of Illinois American Indian organizations. Supports American Indian-owned businesses with resources and tools to grow and succeed in the marketplace.
verification_evidence: Active in 2025-2026 providing business support and legislative advocacy.

name: Newberry Library — D'Arcy McNickle Center
description: The D'Arcy McNickle Center for American Indian and Indigenous Studies, founded 1972, is one of the most significant American Indian and Indigenous studies research centers in the country. Goals include improving scholarship on Indigenous peoples, educating teachers about Native cultures, assisting tribal historians, and providing a scholarly meeting ground. Home of the Indigenous Chicago project.
verification_evidence: Hosting the 2026 NCAIS Graduate Student Conference and 2026 Summer Institute.

name: Saint Kateri Center of Chicago
description: Ministry of the Archdiocese of Chicago dedicated to serving the American Indian Catholic community through faith formation, traditional prayers, and cultural programming. Hosts monthly Sunday Mass (1st & 3rd Sundays), an annual summer Pow Wow, language workshops (Cherokee, Lakota), and cultural events. Provides back-to-school supplies, youth conference participation, and guest speakers for the Native community.
verification_evidence: Specific 2025/2026 calendars not publicly detailed, but continues to serve the community with ongoing programs.

name: Trickster Cultural Center
description: 501(c)(3) Native American-led cultural arts center founded by Joseph Podlasek (LCO Ojibwe). A 10,000 sq ft facility dedicated to Native American culture, healing, and community in the Chicago area. Hosts art exhibitions, film screenings, cultural programming, and serves as a healing space for the intertribal urban Native community of greater Chicago.
verification_evidence: Involved in a May 2026 feasibility study by the Village of Schaumburg; hosted 2026 Summer Youth Pow Wow.

name: Big Brothers Big Sisters of Metropolitan Chicago
description: The Chicago affiliate of America's oldest and largest youth mentoring organization, providing one-to-one mentoring matches ('Bigs' and 'Littles') for almost 2,000 youth annually across Cook, DuPage, and Lake Counties. Bigs commit to meeting their Little at least twice per month for a minimum of one year. Programs include community-based mentoring, school-based mentoring (SBM), and workplace mentoring. Proven model: youth with Bigs are more likely to graduate high school and avoid risky behavior.
verification_evidence: Hosted Live Big 2026 in March and Big Bash in April 2026.

name: Chicago Arts Partnerships in Education (CAPE)
description: Nonprofit (est. 1992) that embeds professional teaching artists in Chicago Public School classrooms to co-teach with teachers, integrating the arts into core academics like math, science, and literacy. CAPE's model — arts as a teaching tool, not just enrichment — is backed by 30+ years of research showing improved academic outcomes, especially for students in under-resourced schools. Volunteers include professional artists, arts educators, and corporate skills volunteers supporting arts-integrated learning projects.
verification_evidence: Participated in the 2026 RE•VER•BER•ATE festival.

name: Chicago HOPES for Kids
description: Nonprofit providing free academic support, tutoring, enrichment, and trauma-informed care to school-age children experiencing homelessness in Chicago shelters and housing programs. Chicago HOPES for Kids sends trained volunteer tutors to shelters every Sunday, Tuesday, and Thursday to provide one-on-one academic support, homework help, and enrichment activities in a safe, nurturing environment. Serves one of Chicago's most vulnerable youth populations — children in emergency shelter.
verification_evidence: Active AmeriCorps recruitment for 2026 terms and hosted 2026 Seeds of HOPE Casino Night.

name: Chicago Lights Tutoring
description: A program of Fourth Presbyterian Church of Chicago providing free one-to-one tutoring and mentoring to youth in grades 3-12 from Chicago's most under-resourced neighborhoods. Chicago Lights pairs each student with a caring adult volunteer tutor who commits to a weekly session. In addition to academic support, the program weaves in mindfulness, career development, and enrichment resources. Volunteer tutors come from across Chicago and beyond — no teaching background required, just a commitment to show up.
verification_evidence: 2025–2026 school year program runs through May 14, 2026.

name: Chicago Scholars
description: Citywide nonprofit supporting first-generation, low-income Chicago Public School students through the complete college journey — from 10th grade through career launch. Chicago Scholars provides mentors, college advising, scholarships, internship connections, and alumni networks to ensure students not only access but graduate from college. Volunteers serve as mentors guiding scholars through college applications, financial aid, the first year on campus, and job searches. Impact: 95%+ college retention rate for Scholar participants.
verification_evidence: Application deadline for the Class of 2031 was February 4, 2026.

name: Chicago Youth Centers (CYC)
description: Community-based youth development nonprofit (est. 1956) operating multiple centers across Chicago's most under-resourced neighborhoods — Englewood, Roseland, Lawndale, Humboldt Park, and others. CYC provides after-school programs, summer camps, sports leagues, mentoring, and college and career readiness for youth ages 6–18. Relies heavily on volunteers for center events, seasonal programming, tutoring, and operational support. Serves 8,000+ youth annually across Chicago's South and West Sides.
verification_evidence: Hosted the annual Shake & Stir cocktail party on February 27, 2026.

name: Lumity
description: Chicago nonprofit dedicated to shaping the future of STEM education and expanding career pathways for Chicagoland youth, particularly those from communities historically underrepresented in STEM. Lumity's signature program, STEMWorks, places Chicago Public School students in paid internships at STEM companies; STEMTalks brings STEM professionals directly into classrooms. Lumity also facilitates career site visits, professional mentoring, and resume workshops — all powered by volunteer professionals from the Chicago STEM community.
verification_evidence: Hosted 2026 Annual Benefit in March and Pours with Purpose in May 2026.

name: Mikva Challenge

Mikva's Chicago programs include Project Soapbox (public speaking), Issues to Action (community organizing and advocacy curriculum), and Elections in Action (youth-led electoral engagement, including serving as student election judges). Partners with Chicago Public Schools to deliver curriculum and professional development. Over 14,000 youth engaged annually across Chicago.

name: One Million Degrees (OMD)

Volunteer coaches serve as thought partners, cheerleaders, and accountability partners for OMD Scholars, meeting a few hours a month. Especially powerful for first-generation college students facing financial and life barriers.
verification_evidence: 2026 Degrees of Impact gala scheduled for May 29, 2026.

name: Project Exploration

Programs include youth-led research, field science experiences, STEM internships, and summer science institutes. Volunteers and mentors who are passionate about science (professional scientists, science communicators, educators) serve as role models and hands-on program supporters for Chicago youth underrepresented in STEM.

name: Project: VISION

One of very few Chicago youth nonprofits specifically centering Asian and Pacific Islander youth in under-resourced South Side communities.
verification_evidence: Actively executing its VISION 2027 strategic plan spanning 2025–2027.

name: Reading In Motion
description: Chicago nonprofit (est. 1983) with a mission to get every at-risk student reading at or above grade level in the early elementary grades through the power and discipline of the arts. Reading In Motion uses an arts-integration model — songs, movement, visual art, and drama — to make phonics and early reading engaging and effective for kindergarten through 3rd grade students in under-resourced Chicago schools. Volunteer literacy coaches work directly with classroom teachers and students in CPS schools.

name: SitStayRead

Children read aloud to dogs — non-judgmental, patient listeners — reducing anxiety, building confidence, and improving fluency. SitStayRead also offers traditional (non-dog) volunteer Book Buddy roles for people without dogs. Partners with Chicago Public Schools and Chicago Public Library. Research-backed model with 20+ years of impact in Chicago communities.

name: We All Code

We All Code operates pop-up classes and workshops across Chicago through volunteer educators and partner venues, putting hands-on coding experience into communities that lack access to computer science education. WITS also provides teacher professional development. Over 5,000 WITS volunteers engage each year, and the program is deployed citywide across dozens of CPS schools.

name: Chicago House

name: Chicago Justice Project (CJP)

CJP investigates police misconduct, mass incarceration, and systemic racial bias in Chicago's criminal legal system. verification_evidence: Active in 2026 with National Expansion and PSTA Ordinance.

name: Circles & Ciphers
description: Hip-hop infused restorative justice and arts organization led by and for young people impacted by violence and incarceration. Chicago's northside restorative justice hub, Circles & Ciphers uses peace circles, creative arts, and mutual aid to help system-impacted youth heal, create, and build power. Runs Kaba Studios offering free/low-cost recording, and a mutual aid program for people navigating reentry.
verification_evidence: Hosted Two Piece Fest Chicago 2026 on Feb 14, 2026.

name: Illinois Prison Project (IPP)
description: Nonprofit organization headquartered in Chicago that reduces mass incarceration through novel legal approaches, public education, and advocacy. IPP provides legal services to people who are incarcerated, works on prison reform campaigns, and engages in policy advocacy statewide. Volunteers support education, outreach, reentry, and policy work.
verification_evidence: Active, 6th Annual Gala scheduled for Oct 16, 2026.

name: John Howard Association of Illinois (JHA)
description: Independent prison watchdog organization that monitors conditions in Illinois prisons, jails, and juvenile facilities since 1901. JHA conducts roughly 20 prison monitoring visits per year and advocates for humane conditions and systemic reform. Volunteers assist with communications, events, and accompany staff on about 15 of those monitoring visits annually.
verification_evidence: 125th Anniversary Celebration planned for Oct 23, 2026.

name: Moms United Against Violence and Incarceration (MUAVI)
description: Intergenerational membership-based abolitionist organization building the collective strength and power of mothers and caregivers directly impacted by racial violence and incarceration in Chicago. MUAVI supports incarcerated caregivers and coordinates community-based defense for moms involved in the criminal legal system. Deeply rooted in Black feminist and abolitionist principles.
verification_evidence: Continues to organize reunification rides and advocacy in 2026.

name: Precious Blood Ministry of Reconciliation (PBMR)
description: Faith-rooted restorative justice organization serving young people, families, and adults in Back of the Yards and Englewood impacted by violence, incarceration, and structural inequity since 2004. Programs include a peace circle process, education lab, transitional jobs, and hospitality for people returning from prison. One of Chicago's leading models for restorative and transformative justice.
verification_evidence: Released Lenten reflection booklet and April 2026 blogs.

name: Prison + Neighborhood Arts/Education Project (PNAP)
description: Arts and education project rooted in solidarity between communities inside and outside prison. PNAP brings teaching artists, educators, and community organizers into Illinois prisons to run workshops and programming, and builds bridges between incarcerated people and Chicago neighborhoods. Partners with academic institutions and Chicago arts community for sustained creative collaboration.
verification_evidence: Ongoing advocacy and academic references in 2026.

name: Restore Justice Foundation
description: Chicago-based nonprofit working to reform sentencing laws, improve prison conditions, and expand pathways to release for Illinois prisoners — especially those serving lengthy or life sentences. name: Safer Foundation

name: Transformative Justice Law Project of Illinois (TJLP)
